title: Learning path: think like a collaborative senior engineer

lesson.goal: Turn interview pressure into clear, evidence-based communication.
lesson.outcome: A two-minute answer structure: context, decision, trade-off, result, and reflection.
lesson.challenge: Answer one difficult question without claiming experience you do not have.

sections.heading: Use one repeatable answer loop
sections.content: Start with the situation and the constraint. State your reasoning, the option you chose, the trade-off you accepted, and what you learned. This prevents both empty confidence and overlong technical monologues.

sections.heading: Practice with evidence
sections.content: For every claim, prepare one concrete example, one limitation, and one question you would ask the interviewer. Good judgment is more convincing than a perfect-sounding answer.

title: Learning path: understand the logistics problem before proposing solutions

lesson.goal: Connect technical choices to transport operations, people, and service outcomes.
lesson.outcome: A concise problem map linking a user journey, operational pain, data, and system boundary.
lesson.challenge: Explain a logistics trade-off without jumping straight to a tool.

sections.heading: Follow one operational journey
sections.content: Trace a delivery from planning to completion. At each step, identify who acts, what data changes, and what happens when a dependency is delayed. This reveals the real system before an architecture diagram does.

sections.heading: Turn domain knowledge into questions
sections.content: Ask about volume, peak periods, SLAs, manual workarounds, data ownership, and customer impact. A relevant solution begins with the cost of the current problem.

title: Learning path: model behaviour, not class diagrams

lesson.goal: Use objects to protect business rules and make change safer.
lesson.outcome: A small model with invariants, responsibilities, and focused tests.
lesson.challenge: Replace one inheritance hierarchy with composition where behaviour varies.

sections.heading: Begin with an invariant
sections.content: Choose a rule that must always hold, such as “a delivery cannot be completed before dispatch”. Put the rule near the state it protects and test the allowed transitions.

sections.heading: Judge the model by change
sections.content: When a new delivery type arrives, ask whether you add a focused collaborator or modify unrelated classes. A good object model localises change.

title: Learning path: use SOLID to reduce change cost

lesson.goal: Recognise coupling that makes ordinary changes risky.
lesson.outcome: One refactoring proposal with a before/after dependency sketch and tests.
lesson.challenge: Explain the trade-off of an abstraction that is not worth creating yet.

sections.heading: Start from a painful change
sections.content: Do not apply principles as a checklist. Choose a feature that forces edits in unrelated code, then identify the responsibility or dependency that caused the ripple.

sections.heading: Prove improvement with tests
sections.content: Refactor in small steps. The result should make the change easier to test, not merely create more interfaces and files.

title: Learning path: choose data structures from operations

lesson.goal: Select Java data structures based on access, ordering, duplication, and cost.
lesson.outcome: A short decision note and a benchmark or test for one hot path.
lesson.challenge: Justify one collection choice without saying “it is faster” vaguely.

sections.heading: Name the operations first
sections.content: Will you search by key, preserve insertion order, remove from both ends, allow duplicates, or process a stream once? The operations narrow the choice before implementation starts.

sections.heading: Validate assumptions
sections.content: Use realistic input sizes and measure only when performance matters. Prefer the simplest structure that satisfies correctness and clarity.

title: Learning path: modern Java for safer, clearer domain code

lesson.goal: Use language features to make invalid states and accidental mutation harder.
lesson.outcome: A small refactoring using records, sealed types, pattern matching, or structured concurrency where appropriate.
lesson.challenge: Remove boilerplate without hiding business meaning.

sections.heading: Choose a real friction point
sections.content: Start with a DTO that should be immutable, a closed set of states, or conditional logic that is hard to read. Apply one feature to make that intent explicit.

sections.heading: Keep compatibility in view
sections.content: Modern syntax is valuable only when the team, build, libraries, and runtime can support it. Record the minimum Java version and migration impact.

title: Learning path: diagnose data access before optimising

lesson.goal: Make query performance and correctness observable.
lesson.outcome: A query plan review, index decision, and safe migration plan.
lesson.challenge: Explain why an index helps one query but may hurt writes.

sections.heading: Start from a user-facing query
sections.content: Capture the exact filters, sort, cardinality, and expected response time. Inspect the execution plan before adding caches or infrastructure.

sections.heading: Treat data changes as releases
sections.content: Use compatible migrations, measure lock and write impact, and keep rollback possible. A fast query that corrupts or blocks production is not an optimisation.

title: Learning path: design from constraints to validation

lesson.goal: Create a defensible system design rather than reciting a reference architecture.
lesson.outcome: Requirements, estimates, diagram, bottlenecks, trade-offs, and validation plan.
lesson.challenge: State what you deliberately do not solve in version one.

sections.heading: Clarify before drawing
sections.content: Ask about users, volume, latency, consistency, data sensitivity, failure tolerance, cost, and team ownership. Estimates are assumptions to make visible, not decoration.

sections.heading: Design a thin end-to-end path
sections.content: Describe request flow, storage, failure behaviour, and observability first. Add cache, queue, sharding, or multi-region only when a constraint makes it necessary.

title: Learning path: show judgment through stories

lesson.goal: Communicate collaboration, conflict, and growth with credible evidence.
lesson.outcome: Three STAR stories with a technical decision and an honest lesson learned.
lesson.challenge: Describe a failure without blaming people or hiding your part.

sections.heading: Use a decision as the centre of the story
sections.content: Situation and task matter, but the interview value is in what you noticed, how you involved others, and why you chose an action.

sections.heading: End with learning
sections.content: A strong story names the outcome and what you would change next time. Reflection signals maturity more than a flawless outcome.

title: Learning path: turn experience into memorable evidence

lesson.goal: Preserve the impact and emotion of real work while making its reasoning clear.
lesson.outcome: A one-minute and a three-minute version of each story.
lesson.challenge: Quantify impact without inventing numbers.

sections.heading: Keep the human and business stakes
sections.content: Explain the manual burden, uncertainty, customer impact, or operational bottleneck that made the work matter before presenting the technology.

sections.heading: Make your contribution precise
sections.content: Separate what you personally designed or implemented from what the team or organisation achieved. This makes the story credible and easy to follow.

title: Learning path: present a project as decisions and proof

lesson.goal: Show how a project solves a problem, not just which frameworks it uses.
lesson.outcome: A project narrative with context, architecture, trade-offs, evidence, and next improvements.
lesson.challenge: Explain one limitation of the project before someone asks.

sections.heading: Lead with value
sections.content: State the user or business problem, the workflow, and the resulting capability. Technology follows as evidence of how the solution was built.

sections.heading: Use artefacts as proof
sections.content: Show a diagram, a small code slice, a test, a dashboard, or an ADR. One concrete artefact is more persuasive than a long technology inventory.

title: Learning path: build a Spring service that is safe to change

lesson.goal: Use Spring as an adapter around clear business rules and observable operations.
lesson.outcome: A vertical feature with tests, validation, transaction boundary, and health signal.
lesson.challenge: Explain where the transaction starts and why.

sections.heading: Build one vertical slice
sections.content: Follow one request from controller through use case and persistence. Keep validation, error mapping, and business rules intentional rather than spread across framework annotations.

sections.heading: Operate what you build
sections.content: Add health, metrics, configuration discipline, and integration tests before calling a service complete. A controller returning 200 is not enough.

title: Learning path: design contracts for consumers and change

lesson.goal: Create APIs that clients can understand, evolve, and recover from.
lesson.outcome: An OpenAPI-backed endpoint with errors, pagination, idempotency, and compatibility rules.
lesson.challenge: Describe what a client should do after a timeout.

sections.heading: Model a consumer journey
sections.content: Start with what a client needs to accomplish, not your database table. Define resource, operation, validation, state transitions, errors, and authorization.

sections.heading: Plan evolution on day one
sections.content: Prefer additive changes, stable error shapes, explicit deprecation, and contract tests. A breaking change is a product event, not just a code change.

title: Learning path: protect a real flow, not a compliance checklist

lesson.goal: Make threats, controls, and operational evidence explicit.
lesson.outcome: A short threat model and security controls for one sensitive flow.
lesson.challenge: Show how a compromised credential is limited and detected.

sections.heading: Trace a sensitive action
sections.content: Follow login, authorization, data access, and audit logging through one user journey. Mark trust boundaries and abuse paths before choosing security products.

sections.heading: Layer controls deliberately
sections.content: Identity, least privilege, input handling, secrets, encryption, rate limits, and monitoring each address different risk. Name the owner and verification for every control.

title: Learning path: use messaging for a business fact

lesson.goal: Design event flows that tolerate replay, duplication, delay, and evolution.
lesson.outcome: A versioned event contract, partition strategy, and consumer recovery plan.
lesson.challenge: Prove a duplicate event cannot create a duplicate business effect.

sections.heading: Begin with a fact
sections.content: Publish an event only when something meaningful happened in a domain. Define its owner, key, ordering need, retention, sensitive data, and consumer contract.

sections.heading: Operate the unhappy path
sections.content: Simulate consumer lag, malformed data, redelivery, and broker outage. A queue is useful only when the team can observe and recover it.

title: Learning path: map persistence without losing performance or invariants

lesson.goal: Make entity design, transaction boundaries, and query behaviour intentional.
lesson.outcome: A mapped aggregate with an integration test and a measured query plan.
lesson.challenge: Find and eliminate one N+1 or lazy-loading surprise.

sections.heading: Start from aggregate behaviour
sections.content: Choose which state changes together and which invariants must hold. Map relationships to support that behaviour, not merely to mirror every foreign key.

sections.heading: Observe generated SQL
sections.content: Use integration tests and logs to inspect joins, fetches, locks, and transaction boundaries. ORM convenience never removes database cost.

title: Learning path: make shared work correct before making it parallel

lesson.goal: Reason about ownership, visibility, cancellation, and failure across concurrent tasks.
lesson.outcome: A concurrency design with a safety invariant and a repeatable test.
lesson.challenge: Explain what happens if one task fails after another has already changed state.

sections.heading: Identify shared mutable state
sections.content: Ask who owns each value and whether it is immutable, confined, synchronised, or externally coordinated. Most concurrency bugs start before an executor is chosen.

sections.heading: Test the failure paths
sections.content: Use timeouts, cancellation, load, and repeated runs. A single successful run proves almost nothing about concurrent correctness.

title: Learning path: use patterns as names for trade-offs

lesson.goal: Select a pattern when a concrete pressure exists, not because the diagram is familiar.
lesson.outcome: A before/after code sketch explaining the pressure, benefit, and cost.
lesson.challenge: Reject one pattern that would add indirection without solving a current problem.

sections.heading: Start with changing behaviour
sections.content: Ask what varies, what should stay stable, and who should own the variation. Then compare a direct implementation with the smallest pattern that isolates it.

sections.heading: Watch for pattern debt
sections.content: Every abstraction adds concepts to learn and paths to debug. Keep it only while its flexibility pays for its complexity.

title: Learning path: create confidence at the right boundary

lesson.goal: Use tests to reduce the risks that matter most in a change.
lesson.outcome: A test strategy for one feature: unit, integration, and end-to-end evidence where justified.
lesson.challenge: Remove a brittle test that proves implementation detail rather than behaviour.

sections.heading: Test decisions, not lines
sections.content: Start with invariants, public contracts, failure handling, and integration points. The right test makes a risky change safe to make.

sections.heading: Choose the cheapest credible signal
sections.content: Unit tests are fast; integration tests expose real wiring; end-to-end tests prove journeys but cost more. Balance feedback speed with the risk being covered.

title: Learning path: package a service for repeatable delivery

lesson.goal: Make local, CI, and production execution predictable.
lesson.outcome: A small, reproducible image with configuration, health, and secret boundaries defined.
lesson.challenge: Explain why the image is safe to rebuild and does not contain a production secret.

sections.heading: Make the runtime explicit
sections.content: Specify base image, build stage, ports, filesystem ownership, configuration inputs, and health behaviour. A container image is an operational contract.

sections.heading: Validate outside your laptop
sections.content: Build from a clean environment, run with non-default configuration, scan dependencies, and confirm the image starts without hidden local state.

title: Learning path: declare workload behaviour and recovery

lesson.goal: Use Kubernetes to express availability, configuration, and safe rollout requirements.
lesson.outcome: A deployment with resources, probes, configuration, and a rollback-aware rollout plan.
lesson.challenge: Explain what Kubernetes does when the application is alive but cannot serve customers.

sections.heading: Model the workload first
sections.content: Define statelessness, dependencies, startup time, capacity, persistence, and failure modes before writing YAML. Manifests should encode those facts.

sections.heading: Test deployment failure
sections.content: Verify readiness, liveness, scaling, configuration change, and rollback in a controlled environment. Kubernetes does not make an unhealthy application healthy.

title: Learning path: express domain intent with Kotlin safely

lesson.goal: Use Kotlin features to reduce null, mutability, and boilerplate mistakes.
lesson.outcome: A small domain model with explicit nullability, immutable data, and focused tests.
lesson.challenge: Interoperate with one Java API without hiding platform-null risk.

sections.heading: Start with an invalid state
sections.content: Identify a value that should not be null or a state that should not mutate. Use Kotlin’s type system and data modelling to make the rule visible.

sections.heading: Keep JVM boundaries explicit
sections.content: Java interoperability, framework proxies, serialization, and exceptions can weaken Kotlin guarantees. Test those edges rather than assuming language safety transfers automatically.

title: Learning path: build an observable, accessible user journey

lesson.goal: Use Angular structure to make UI state, data flow, and error handling understandable.
lesson.outcome: One accessible feature with a state model, service boundary, and failure UI.
lesson.challenge: Show what the user sees while data loads, fails, and retries.

sections.heading: Model state before components
sections.content: List loading, success, empty, validation, permission, and failure states. Components and signals should make these states easy to see and test.

sections.heading: Follow the user journey
sections.content: Trace route, input, service call, rendering, and recovery. Performance and accessibility belong in the feature definition, not in a final polish pass.
